sound_type: sonic crack at speed -- aerodynamic acoustic event produced by falcon body displacing air at 200-240 mph (320-386 km/h); compressed-air crack from leading edges + feather wingcut sound; not a vocalization but the acoustic consequence of maximum velocity; broadband compressed-air character; heard within 50-100 meters of stoop trajectory
ecosystem: Open sky -- the acoustic event occurs in the aerial column above any habitat type where Peregrine hunts; cliff faces, urban structures (building ledges as cliff analogs), coastline, open country; foraging habitat wherever bird prey concentrates
season: Resident -- Peregrine Falcons resident in Pacific Northwest year-round; migratory F. p. tundrius passes through spring and fall; urban pairs active year-round on Seattle and Portland building ledges
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN globally). Removed from US Federal Endangered Species List 1999 after DDT recovery. Pacific Northwest populations recovered. Urban pairs established in Seattle, Portland, Tacoma. F. p. pealei (outer coast subspecies) never fully extirpated; F. p. anatum (continental) recovered through captive breeding program.
milestone: Return to Falconidae at degree 296. FIRST non-vocalization aerodynamic acoustic event in SPS engine. The acoustic consequence of velocity rather than the acoustic consequence of communicative intent. E=8 -- highest energy in current arc sequence.

coordinates.description: Seattle urban pairs on downtown building ledges. Coastal cliff sites throughout Pacific Northwest. Open country during migration. Tatoosh Island (Cape Flattery) for F. p. pealei. Year-round presence.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: NOT A VOCALIZATION. Aerodynamic acoustic event produced by bird's body displacing air at maximum velocity. Compressed-air crack + feather wingcut at 200-240 mph.
acoustic_data.stoop_sound: Loud whoosh or crack heard within 50-100 meters of stoop trajectory. Duration: under 1 second of full-velocity event at point of closest approach. Broadband character: not tonal frequency like vocalization but compressed-air burst.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: Broadband. Not a tonal frequency. The acoustic character is percussive and compressed: a crack rather than a tone. The physics of air displacement at 240 mph rather than oscillating vocal cords.
acoustic_data.wake_turbulence: Diminishing whoosh as displaced air returns to equilibrium after falcon passes. The complete event: silence (vertical approach from height) -- CRACK (terminal velocity) -- diminishing turbulence (wake).
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8: the highest energy in current arc. Not loud in sustained sense -- it is brief, decisive, absolute. E=8 reflects the velocity, the absolute commitment of the event, the sudden total physical presence. Arrives at E=8 because it is physically absolute: no half-measures, no gradations.

nodes.title: Fastest Animal on Earth -- 240 mph Stoop
nodes.description: Peregrine Falcon's terminal stoop velocity of 200-240 mph (320-386 km/h) is the fastest measured movement of any animal on Earth. No other species is close in the same velocity category: spine-tailed swift ~106 mph in level flight, cheetah ~70 mph on ground. Adaptations: nasal tubercle deflects high-pressure air intake, nictitating membrane protects eyes, streamlined folded-wing profile, notched kill-bill for cervical strike.

nodes.title: First Non-Vocalization Aerodynamic Sound in SPS Engine
nodes.description: The Peregrine Falcon stoop is the first SPS entry documenting a non-vocalization sound generated by bird movement rather than communicative intent. All previous bird entries documented vocalizations or structural sounds (drumming, wingbeats-in-context). The stoop wingcut is the acoustic consequence of physics: air displacement at 240 mph. A new acoustic category enters the engine at degree 296.

nodes.title: DDT Recovery -- Delisted 1999
nodes.description: Peregrine Falcon extirpated from most of continental US east of Rockies by DDT-induced eggshell thinning by early 1970s. 1972 DDT ban + Cornell/Peregrine Fund captive breeding and release program. Delisted from Federal ESA 1999: one of American conservation's most successful raptor recoveries. Urban Seattle pairs breeding on building ledges are descendants of recovery program birds.

nodes.title: Predator-Prey Context -- Rhinoceros Auklet 295
nodes.description: Rhinoceros Auklet's strict nocturnality at colony sites (degree 295) evolved partly as Peregrine Falcon avoidance. Engine places the prey's adaptation (nocturnal colony moans, 295) immediately before the predator's maximum-performance event (stoop wingcut, 296). The ecological logic is preserved in the engine's consecutive degree structure: the prey's solution, then the predator's event.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=8 = E=15. The pivot point of Seattle grunge history (Wood's death generates Temple of the Dog and Pearl Jam) pairs with the world's fastest animal at peak velocity. Both are moments of absolute commitment. Both pivot what comes after.
Prey-predator consecutive pair. The auklet's nocturnality is partly evolved Peregrine avoidance. The engine sequences them consecutively: prey adaptation at 295, predator event at 296.

Return to Falconidae at degree 296 with the family's defining behavioral event: not a call or contact vocalization but the acoustic consequence of maximum velocity. The family represented at its most extreme behavioral expression.
